message:
feat(server/dispatch): cooperative cancellation of blocking MCP dispatch work on timeout, with permit hold until the worker actually exits

The old dispatch path used timeout() + spawn_blocking(). When the
timeout fired, the outer future dropped the spawn_blocking join handle
and the request returned an error — but the blocking worker kept
running, holding the dispatch permit, until it completed on its own.
Under contention that meant "timeout" was a user-visible error that
did nothing to relieve pressure: the concurrent-request budget stayed
full, back-pressure kept mounting, and every subsequent request
inherited the backlog.

This change threads a cooperative cancellation token into the blocking
work and releases the permit only after the worker has acknowledged it.

- New DispatchCancel wraps an `Arc<AtomicBool>` plus the request Cx.
  `request(&cx)` flips the bit with Release ordering AND calls
  `cx.set_cancel_requested(true)` so any `Cx::checkpoint()` already
  threaded through downstream helpers fails fast. `is_requested()`
  reads it with Acquire. `check()` returns the standard
  `Request cancelled` McpError when set.
- New dispatch_checkpoint(cx, cancel) helper composes cancel.check()
  with cx.checkpoint(), giving callers a single "safe to continue?"
  call they can sprinkle through long DB work.
- New dispatch_cancelled_error() and dispatch_timeout_error(method,
  secs) centralize the two error shapes so the message contract is
  in one place.
- New run_cancellable_blocking_dispatch<T, F>(method,
  dispatch_timeout_secs, dispatch_cx, cancel, work) replaces the
  inline timeout+spawn_blocking block. It:
    * spawns the blocking closure with a clone of the cancel token,
    * awaits a timeout against wall_now(),
    * on elapse: calls cancel.request(&dispatch_cx) to flip both the
      atomic AND the Cx, logs a tracing::error! with method and
      timeout secs, and returns dispatch_timeout_error,
    * treats dispatch_timeout_secs == 0 as "no timeout" and simply
      awaits the spawn to completion (matches the request_timeout_secs
      "infinite budget" semantic from the old path).
  The permit is moved into the closure so it's held for the full
  duration of the blocking work, even past the outer timeout — which
  is the point: the permit is what bounds DISPATCH_INFLIGHT and we
  must not release it until the worker actually exits.
- Two new tests:
    * dispatch_timeout_requests_blocking_work_cancellation — starts a
      30 s polling worker with a 1 s dispatch timeout. Asserts the
      outer result is an error within ~3 s, the Cx is marked cancelled,
      the worker's cancel.is_requested() loop observes the flip, and
      the worker returns within the 5 s grace window.
    * The existing permit-holds-until-blocking-work-exits test is
      extended to verify DISPATCH_INFLIGHT stays at `before` until
      release_worker is flipped, then drops to before-1 — i.e. the
      permit survives the cancellation and only releases when the
      blocking work itself exits.

Result: timed-out requests now actively relieve back-pressure instead
of merely reporting failure while the underlying work wedges on.
